lesson: c4-l01 / AI and Accessibility — An Overview
title: AI and Accessibility — An Overview

scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: AI and accessibility. [pause] It does not solve inclusion on its own. [pause] But it can remove concrete barriers — in the moment they appear.

scenes.name: The gap
scenes.text: Traditional accommodations work — but they require planning, equipment, and people. [pause] When the assistant is absent, when the translation hasn't arrived, the gap returns. [pause] That gap is where learning is lost.

scenes.name: Four areas
scenes.text: AI helps in four core areas. [pause] Vision. Hearing. Cognitive processing. [pause] And language. [pause] Each represents a different kind of barrier — and a different kind of bridge.

scenes.name: Real-time
scenes.text: The principle behind all four is <emphasis>real-time</emphasis> adaptation. [pause] Traditional supports require preparation in advance. [pause] AI can adapt content at the moment of need — when a topic is introduced, when a question is asked, when a worksheet appears.

scenes.name: Tool inventory
scenes.text: The toolkit is wider than you might think. [pause] ChatGPT, Claude, Otter, Immersive Reader, Goblin Tools, ARASAAC — most run on a phone the student already has.

scenes.name: Limits
scenes.text: AI cannot assess individual needs. [pause] It cannot judge whether an adaptation is pedagogically appropriate. [pause] Transcripts have errors. Translations miss nuance. [pause] Every output needs human review.

scenes.name: Teacher role
scenes.text: Your role has three functions. [pause] Gatekeeper — deciding when AI is appropriate. [pause] Quality controller — reviewing the adapted output. [pause] And learning designer — fitting the tool into the lesson.

scenes.name: Starting point
scenes.text: This is a starting point. Not a complete solution. [pause] But it represents real, practical access that did not exist before — at the moments when prepared adaptations are not yet there.
